name: Tipua
description: Tipua are supernatural guardians or spirits in Māori mythology, often associated with unusual or prominent natural features such as rocks, trees, or pools of water. They can be malevolent or benevolent, depending on their individual nature and how they are treated. Tipua often possess immense power and are capable of causing great misfortune or offering protection and guidance. They are considered to be ancient beings, deeply connected to the land and the ancestors. Disrespecting a place considered to be the dwelling of a Tipua can lead to illness, misfortune, or even death. It is believed that Tipua can manifest in various forms, including animals, inanimate objects, or even as human-like figures. Their presence serves as a reminder of the spiritual significance of the natural world and the importance of respecting the traditions and beliefs of the Māori people. Tipua are a vital part of the Māori worldview, emphasizing the interconnectedness of the physical and spiritual realms, and the enduring power of the ancestral spirits. They represent both the dangers and the blessings that can be found in the natural world, and the need for careful consideration and respect when interacting with it. Their stories serve as cautionary tales and guides for living in harmony with the environment and honoring the past.
subtitle: Guardians of sacred places
appearance: Tipua can manifest in various forms, often appearing as unusual or distinctive natural features like strangely shaped rocks, ancient trees with peculiar markings, or pools of water with unnatural colors. They can also appear as animals, such as lizards, birds, or fish, or even as human-like figures, often with glowing eyes or an otherworldly aura. Their appearance is often tied to the specific place they guard, reflecting the unique characteristics of that location.
habitat: Tipua reside in and are deeply connected to specific natural locations considered sacred or significant in Māori tradition. These include prominent rocks, ancient trees, pools of water, caves, mountains, and other places with unusual or distinctive features. Their presence is often associated with areas of great natural beauty or spiritual power.
behavior: Tipua can be benevolent or malevolent, depending on their individual nature and how they are treated. They act as guardians of their specific locations, protecting them from disrespect or harm. Benevolent Tipua may offer guidance, protection, and blessings to those who show respect and reverence. Malevolent Tipua may inflict misfortune, illness, or even death upon those who disrespect their domain. Their behavior is often unpredictable and influenced by the actions of those who interact with their domain.
weaknesses: Destruction or desecration of their sacred place

keyMyths.title: The Tipua Stone
keyMyths.description: A large, unusually shaped stone is considered a Tipua. Locals tell stories of misfortune befalling those who attempt to move or deface the stone, highlighting the Tipua's power.
keyMyths.source: Oral tradition of the local iwi

keyMyths.title: Guardian of the Hot Springs
keyMyths.description: A Tipua is believed to reside within a geothermal hot spring. It protects the spring's healing properties and punishes those who pollute or misuse its waters.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell
sources.work: The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Māori mythology, including descriptions of various creatures and spirits.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Māori mythology, including descriptions of various creatures and spirits.
